data.title: InPlay Oil: Cardium's Acquisition Will Unlock Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant InPlay Oil (OTCQX:IPOOF) (TSX:IPO:CA) a buy rating. I consider that there are substantial grounds to believe that InPlay will continue to generate value for its investors. To begin with, the new assets that the company acquired from Obsidian Energy (OBE) in Cardium will unlock value. This acquisition is expected to increase InPlay's production from 8,900 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boe/d) to approximately 18,750 boe/d. Secondly, these assets have a 22% decline rate, which is lower than the 26% decline rate of the other InPlay assets. Thirdly, the proven reserves increased by more than 100%. With the new acquisition, InPlay became the largest oil producer in the Cardium formation. As such, the company will benefit from economies of scale. A qualitative factor to mention is that the CEO of InPlay possesses significant experience as a CEO in the Cardium formation.

The long-term debt of InPlay increased from CA$63.13 million in Q2 2024 to CA$240.28 million in Q2 2025, which is a risk factor. That quarter, InPlay had a 66.46% liabilities-to-assets ratio. Furthermore, the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects lower oil prices for this year. It projects that Brent crude oil will decline to $51 per barrel in 2025. If this occurs, the company may face difficulties repaying its debt. When pondering all positive and negative aspects of InPlay, I consider that a buy rating fits well for the company.

The Company

InPlay is a Canadian company headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, engaged in the exploration, development, and production of oil and natural gas. The company focuses on acquiring low-decline assets with a high profit margin. Additionally, it employs techniques to recover more oil from its wells.

In the first six months of 2025, 47% of InPlay's production was crude oil, 40% natural gas, and the remaining 13% NGL, as depicted in the left chart below. However, 80% of the revenues came from crude oil, as this commodity is more valuable, as depicted in the right chart below.

History

The beginning of InPlay dates back to June 2013. On November 7, 2016, InPlay, which was a private company at the time, merged with Anderson Energy. As a consequence of that acquisition, the company acquired assets in the Cardium formation of the Pembina region of Alberta. The merger brought benefits to InPlay, with the most notable one being the increase in the company's production. In the last quarter of 2016, the production of InPlay averaged 2,712 boe/d, increasing to 3,972 boe/d in 2017, representing a 46.46% gain.

As observed in the following chart, InPlay's daily production increased by 69.54% in the first half of 2025 compared to 2024, representing the second-highest annual increase since 2016. This considerable increase was due to the acquisition of light oil assets from OBE, located in the Cardium formation in Pembina.

The acquisition will play a crucial role for InPlay. In a February 2025 presentation, InPlay said that the new Pembina Cardium assets will increase its production from 8,900 boe/d to approximately 18,750 boe/d. As such, its oil production is expected to increase by more than 100%. The liquid oil assets accounted for 61% of the new assets' production, compared to 40% of oil liquid production that InPlay had prior to the acquisition. Consequently, InPlay will produce more crude oil as a percentage of its total production, which is positive news. To provide context, in Q2 2025, the company sold each boe of oil for CA$81.01, while its natural gas production was sold for CA$1.83 per thousand cubic feet (Mcf), or approximately CA$10.98 per boe.

InPlay's new assets also have a lower decline rate. The decline rate is how much, in percentage terms, a well will lose per year. For instance, if a well produces 100 barrels of oil per day, and after a year it produces 70 barrels of oil per day, it indicates that its decline rate is 30%. With the previous explanation in mind, InPlay's new assets have a 22% decline rate, an improvement from the 26% that it had before the acquisition. As such, InPlay will have lower depletion.

With the new acquisition, InPlay increased its oil and natural gas reserves. In 2023, the company reported proved reserves of 43,911.7 boe, increasing to 96,423 boe after the acquisition of OBE's assets.

CEO

In any organization, the CEO is an important figure. His experience and skills are a determining factor for many firms. For this reason, this section analyzes the background of InPlay's CEO.

Before joining InPlay, Douglas J. Bartole was the president and CEO of Vero Energy Inc. He led the company from September 2005 to November 2012. In the first nine months of 2008, Vero Energy averaged a daily production of 6,032 boe, declining to 2,291 boe/d over the first nine months of 2012, representing a 62.02% decrease. Vero Energy sold its natural gas assets in January 2012. With this divestiture, the company experienced a significant decline in its production. The financial resources generated from the divestiture were used to pay debt. However, Vero Energy saw a drastic reduction in its production after the sale. Consequently, I think that Mr Bartole's performance at Vero Energy could be considered less than successful between 2008 and 2012.

In November 2016, Mr. Bartole took office as the CEO of InPlay, a position that he has held until the present. As illustrated in the following chart, the company produced 1,940 boe/d in 2016, increasing to 14,770 boe/d in the first half of 2025, for a whopping 661.34% increase over that period.

Obsidian Energy

I have written two articles about OBE. Therefore, I think that explaining the reasons that led Obsidian to sell its Cardium assets to InPlay will provide another perspective.

In September 2023, OBE had an expansion plan, which consisted of increasing its Peace Reaver's oil production from 8,500 boe/d in 2024 to 26,000 boe/d in 2026. However, its long-term debt increased significantly to implement its expansion plan. It needed financial resources to fund its operations. As such, to reduce its debt level, OBE sold its Cardium assets to InPlay to pay off debt. In fact, its long-term debt declined from CA$332.4 million in Q2 2024 to CA$174.4 million in Q2 2025, representing a decrease of CA$158 million.

In 2024, OBE recorded a CA$395.4 million non-cash impairment since it classified some of its Cardium assets as assets held for sale. In other words, OBE sold its Cardium assets with a discount of CA$395.4 million of the fair value estimated by the company.

OBE seems to be in a hurry to obtain financial resources. In this circumstance, InPlay acquired OBE's Cardium assets. As such, I think that InPlay acquired these assets at an excellent moment.

Cardium

I have mentioned the word Cardium several times throughout this article. In fact, the Cardium formation plays an important role for InPlay. For instance, the merger between InPlay and Anderson Energy was aimed at focusing the operations in Cardium. Moving to the most recent acquisition, InPlay acquired assets from OBE, located in Cardium. As shown in the following chart, the newly acquired assets are located near the other InPlay assets located in Cardium. With the new acquisition, InPlay became the largest oil producer in Cardium.

In addition to previous information, Vero Energy concentrated its operations in Cardium. As commented on in this article, the current CEO of InPlay, Douglas J. Bartole, was the CEO of Vero Energy. He led the company for approximately seven years. In addition, he has served as CEO of InPlay for nearly 13 years. Consequently, he has significant experience in the Cardium formation.

As stated previously, InPlay is now the largest oil producer in the Cardium formation. Consequently, it could benefit from potential synergies that the new lands could provide. With more assets in Cardium, the company may benefit from economies of scale. InPlay estimates that the synergies will reduce its costs by CA$15 million annually. To provide context, InPlay reported revenues of CA$79.84 million in the second quarter of 2025.

Debt

As some readers might have already suspected, InPlay contracted debt to finance its recent acquisition that strengthened its assets in Cardium. In 2024, the company had long-term debt of CA$63.13 million, increasing to CA$240.28 million in Q2 2025. Furthermore, the company's decommissioning obligations increased from CA$90.81 million in Q2 2024 to CA$464.49 million in Q2 2025. The agreement between InPlay and OBE stipulates that the former company will pay these obligations.

The following chart shows InPlay's liabilities-to-assets ratio. This ratio fluctuated between 35% and 40% from 2022 to 2024, then jumped to 66.46% in Q2 2025. This increase was due to the new assets acquired in Cardium.

However, the company expects a 1.1-1.3 net debt-to-EBITDA ratio by the end of 2025. Furthermore, it expects to reduce its net debt by CA$35 million by the end of the year. To provide context, in Q2 2025, the company reported an EBITDA of CA$36.62 million, and its production was 20,401 boe/d. In that period, the WTI averaged $64.57 per barrel. Assuming the company will achieve a similar EBITDA each quarter over the next year, InPlay's total EBITDA would reach CA$146.47 million. Therefore, I think that the company could significantly reduce its long-term debt.

Q2 2025

This section analyzes InPlay's financial results in Q2 2025. This quarter's report will show the results post-acquisition.

In Q2 2025, the company reported CA$84.38 million in revenues, representing a 123.69% increase compared to the same period of 2024. However, it had a net loss of CA$3.19 million in the same period, but this loss was due to CA$10.14 million in transaction and integration costs associated with its Cardium acquisition. These costs will not be recurrent. Without these expenses, the company would have achieved a positive income.

The following table shows InPlay's costs as a proportion of its revenues. In Q2 2025, the operating expenses represented 33% of its revenues, an increase of 200 basis points compared to Q2 2024. Additionally, depreciation and amortization accounted for 36% of the company's revenues in Q2 2025, representing a 500-basis-point increase compared to the same period in 2024. Since Q2 2025 is the first quarter that incorporates the new operations in Cardium, it is still too early to judge InPlay.

Peer Analysis

This section conducts a peer analysis of InPlay, OBE, Bonterra (OTCPK:BNEFF), Saturn Oil & Gas (OTCQX:OILSF), Whitecap Resources (OTCPK:WCPRF), Cenovus (CVE), Spartan Delta Corp. (OTCPK:DALXF), and Yangarra Resources (OTCPK:YGRAF). These companies produce oil in the Cardium formation. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, InPlay's stock price increased by 24.20%, the second-highest value in the peer group. Its stock price also achieved an excellent result over a five-year period, with a 1362.89% increase.

Moving to the valuation ratios, InPlay had higher ratios than their respective medians in the peer group. These ratios suggest that the company is overvalued compared to its peers. For instance, the analyst consensus expects a 4 EV/EBITDA ratio for InPlay, while the median is 3.34.

On the other hand, InPlay has a positive growth outlook. For this year, the analyst consensus expects a 25.31% increase. Since the company implemented a significant asset acquisition in Cardium, I believe the previous figure fell short. In addition, the analyst consensus expects a 21.78% EBITDA growth, the highest value among its peers.

The profit margin suggests that InPlay is less profitable than its peers. For example, the company has a net loss of 2.09%, the third-lowest value in the peer group. I think a critical point is the depreciation and amortization expenses of InPlay. The company should reduce these expenses if it intends to be profitable. For instance, WCPRF has 63.72% and 32.98% EBITDA and EBIT margins, respectively. Meanwhile, InPlay has EBITDA and EBIT margins of 52.22% and 9.44%, respectively.

From a debt perspective, InPlay has significant debt levels. In fact, its debt-to-equity ratio is the second-highest value among its peers. This high value is due to its recent asset acquisition in Cardium. Albeit, the company expects to reduce its debt levels.

Finally, the analyst consensus expects an 8.58% dividend yield for InPlay. I believe this is an appealing value. Additionally, over the past four years, the company has paid an average dividend yield of 5.81%.

Overall, I consider that InPlay has an excellent perspective. For this year, the company has an excellent growth outlook. Additionally, the company is expected to yield a dividend of 8.58%. I believe that these values indicate that the company will likely continue to create value for its investors.

Risk

Oil prices have declined. In 2024, the WTI oil price averaged $76.63 per barrel, declining to $67.49 per barrel from January 1 to September 15 of this year. For 2025, the EIA projects that Brent crude oil will average $51 per barrel. The Brent oil price tends to trade at a higher price than the WTI oil price. As such, the EIA projection suggests that the WTI oil price would decline below $51. Ultimately, InPlay's revenues and net income will be negatively affected.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I consider InPlay to be an excellent investment opportunity. I think that the recent acquisition of InPlay, located in Cardium, will generate value for investors. This acquisition is expected to increase InPlay's production from 8,900 boe/d to approximately 18,750 boe/d. Further, the proved reserves increased from 43,911.7 boe before the acquisition to 96,423 boe after the acquisition. Additionally, the new assets have a 22% decline rate, whereas InPlay's decline rate prior to the acquisition was 26%.

The CEO of InPlay has approximately 20 years of experience leading oil and gas companies in the Cardium formation. I think that his experience could be vital for InPlay's future performance. Additionally, with the new acquisition, the company became the largest oil producer in the Cardium region. As such, its operations in that location could generate synergy due to economies of scale.

InPlay incurred significant debt levels to acquire more assets in Cardium. In the peer analysis section, it has the second-highest debt-to-equity ratio among its peers. This high debt level represents a risk factor. In addition, the EIA projects lower oil prices for 2026. If oil prices decline considerably, InPlay could experience financial distress due to its high debt levels. Nonetheless, despite these risks, I still consider InPlay to be an excellent investment opportunity.

data.title: AMG Veritas Global Focus Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG Veritas Global Focus Fund (Class N) returned 5.20% for the second quarter of 2025, trailing the 11.47% return for its primary benchmark, the MSCI (MSCI) World Index. For the 12-month period ending June 30, 2025, the Fund returned 13.16%, compared with a 16.26% return for the Index.

Market Overview

Global equity markets continued to be dominated by a narrow set of mega-cap technology stocks during Q2 2025. The MSCI World Index rose 11.47% in U.S. Dollar terms, driven by enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) investment and infrastructure spending. Underneath the surface, however, market breadth remained historically narrow, with gains heavily concentrated on a small number of U.S. technology leaders. While many investors continue to focus on rapid AI-driven growth, we see clear parallels with prior capital cycles, most notably the internet infrastructure built out of the late 1990s. In that period, substantial capital poured into "picks and shovels" providers, only to be followed by severe declines as supply outstripped demand and revenue models proved less resilient than hoped. We believe a similar risk dynamic exists today within AI infrastructure, where high capital intensity, uncertain monetisation paths, and emerging in-house solutions by major buyers create meaningful long-term uncertainties. At the same time, more defensive or less "AI-centric" sectors, including health care, have been left behind despite robust long-term fundamentals. Large parts of the health care market continue to face short-term headwinds, including destocking, muted Chinese demand, and shifts in government health care budgets. Yet, we believe these pressures are cyclical rather than structural, and the secular growth drivers tied to ageing populations and rising global health care demand remain firmly in place.

Investment Review

The AMG Veritas Global Focus Fund rose 5.2% in Q2 2025, underperforming the MSCI World Index's 11.47% return. This relative underperformance was primarily driven by our underweight to the largest U.S. technology stocks and our exposure to select health care holdings. Our positions in Microsoft®, Amazon (AMZN).com®, and Alphabet ((GOOG) (GOOGL)) contributed positively as they continued to build on their robust core businesses while carefully integrating AI opportunities in a disciplined way. These remain core long-term holdings given their strong balance sheets, large user ecosystems, and ability to self-fund innovation. Conversely, our health care holdings detracted from performance over the quarter. Names such as UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) and Becton Dickinson (BDX) faced renewed investor caution amid sector-specific pressures. We view these near-term challenges as temporary and believe that deferred returns, rather than foregone ones, will ultimately reward patient investors. Our decision to add to UnitedHealth Group reflects this conviction, supported by its differentiated scale advantages and long-term earnings power. While the market's excitement around AI continues to shape headlines and valuations, we are steadfast in our absolute return discipline. Our focus remains on high-quality, cashgenerative businesses with sustainable competitive advantages and pricing power, not simply those best positioned to ride current narratives.

Looking Ahead

Equity markets will likely remain driven by narrow leadership in the near term, but as capital cycles mature, history suggests that breadth tends to improve, and capital shifts back towards underappreciated areas of the market. We believe our balanced exposure to durable growth franchises and select opportunities away from consensus enthusiasm positions the Fund to deliver attractive long-term returns with lower risk. We continue to prioritise resilience and fundamental strength over index-chasing or speculative trends and remain confident that this patient approach will create enduring value for our investors.

data.title: AutoZone: Stalled EPS Causes Breakdown, Creating Dip Opportunity
data.description:
As strange as it might sound, I think that investors spend too much time worrying about cash flows and profits. Of course, over the long haul, cash flows are king, but in the short term, profit margins and whatnot can move rapidly, and low profitability today could evolve into high profitability next year. I mention that because AutoZone (NYSE:AZO) took a sharp stumble after its quarterly EPS fell short of expectations, coming in at just $48.71 rather than $50.72 as expected. Share prices have stumbled since, but I believe the price decline will prove short-lived and that, fundamentally, AutoZone is a solid investment. Further, while the quarterly report spooked investors, I believe the results delivered some good news and positive developments, which must be taken into account.

AutoZone’s Quarterly Results Are Better Than They Seem

Yes, AZO failed to hit EPS estimates. In fact, the company has a regular habit of falling short, with the previous three EPS estimates failing to match expectations. This time around, a $80 million Last In, First Out (LIFO) charge took a bite out of profits. LIFO charges can be relatively common during periods of high inflation, and with many autoparts facing tariffs, AZO’s Cost of Goods Sold has been under pressure. Still, while we can’t ignore the impact of inflation on AutoZone’s finances, as I discuss later, inflation could actually provide some tailwinds. Revenues were in line with expectations, hitting $6.24 billion in the 4th quarter. On a constant currency basis, sales were up 5.1%. Last year, the 4th quarter had an extra week, but when that extra week was included, sales were actually up 6.9% YOY. Operating expenses as a percentage of sales rose from 31.6% last year to 32.4%.

On the whole, these quarterly results are pretty strong. Yes, the LIFO charge hit profits this quarter, and rising prices might result in similar charges in the future. Still, same-store sales are growing at a solid clip, and the company is adding more stores. Revenues should continue to climb in the months and years ahead, and in the near term, I believe that’s more important than profitability. By maximizing its footprint and focusing on sales growth, AutoZone can secure its position in the market, and later on down the road, can shift focus to profitability.

Expensive Cars Will Stay On the Road Longer

The price of automobiles has been steadily rising in the United States with prices starting to skyrocket in 2021. In August of this year, prices moved up sharply again, after cooling off a bit. With cars and car parts from South Korea, Japan, and other countries now being hit with tariffs, we can expect prices to continue to increase in the future. It’s also important not to overlook insurance costs.

People buying expensive, brand-new cars will also need to budget in insurance. In many cases, cars that are a few years older are cheaper to insure. This is because the car itself simply isn’t as valuable compared to a similar, newer model. If tariffs do push car prices upward, this could also raise insurance costs. (In some cases, safety and antitheft features may reduce the cost of insurance premiums.) Cheaper insurance premiums could encourage people to stick with their older model cars.

In 2025, the average age of cars on the road (USA) came in at a record high of 12.8 years. Ultimately, older cars are more likely to need replacement parts and often require a bit more to maintain. For companies like AutoZone that sell replacement parts and maintenance considerations, older cars should drum up more revenue.

AZO: A Comparative Fundamental Analysis and Valuation

AutoZone has a relatively high GAAP TTM PE ratio at ~28 (Seeking Alpha data). Admittedly, such high PE ratios more commonly belong to tech companies and fast-growing, young market entrants. AZO is an older, more established company, which makes the PE ratio a bit tougher to swallow. Yet as mentioned earlier, I’m more concerned about growth and market penetration. EPS can come at a later time, in my opinion.

As for competitors, Advanced Auto Parts’ (NYSE:AAP) GAAP-PE TTM is running in the red, and its forward PE ratio is 31.75. O’Reilly (NASDAQ:ORLY), meanwhile, has a PE ratio of ~37, which suggests that AZO offers a fairly decent PE ratio. Price/Sales is also rather high for AZO at 3.67. O’Reilly, however, has a PS ratio of 5.21, while Advanced Auto Parts is a tempting .41. Unfortunately for AAP, revenues are on the decline, shrinking 4.85% YOY, while both AZO and ORLY are growing at 5.15%.

The median sector (consumer discretionary) PS ratio is a mere .98%. PS ratios below 1 are often viewed as being a great bargain. For AZO to reach a 1 PS ratio, revenues will have to grow to $69 billion. At its current revenue growth rate (5.15%), it’d take AZO 26 years to top $69 billion in revenue. However, with AZO continuing to add locations, revenue growth may accelerate. Further, AZO doesn’t necessarily need to reach a PS ratio of 1 or less. A PS ratio of 2 is generally considered good in many industries and sectors and would take less time to achieve.

Seeking Alpha's quant tools give AZO a hold rating, with both the valuation and growth metrics getting poor marks. This is a pretty fair assessment, but the sliding share price will lead to a more favorable valuation.

Why I Could Be Wrong

AZO is facing stiff competition from ORLY and AAP, and those aren’t the only competitors. Amazon and other merchant websites have started selling car parts online. An online-only operation may be able to lower costs, which then could be passed on to customers. These risks can’t be ignored, although people may prefer talking with a real, live human to ensure that they got the correct part and to see if he or she has any tips. The evaluation is also rather high, and I suspect that a steep dip could set off a sort of avalanche

Key Takeaway: A Solid, Albeit Expensive Workhorse Stock

I believe AZO will enjoy tailwinds in the months ahead, and thus rate AutoZone as a buy. That said, I actually prefer Advanced Auto Parts owing to its low share price and excellent PS ratio. The risks are higher with AAP than with AZO, but the upside seems substantially larger as well. If you’d like to check out my AAP analysis, click here.

data.title: AMG Veritas China Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG Veritas China Fund (Class N) returned 0.33% for the second quarter of 2025, trailing the 2.01% return for its primary benchmark, the MSCI (MSCI) China Index. For the 12-month period ending June 30, 2025, the Fund returned 14.94%, compared with a 33.78% return for the Index.

Market Overview

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by heightened volatility as geopolitical tensions escalated, with U.S. tariff announcements triggering retaliatory measures from China. While this initially weighed on sentiment, markets found support mid-quarter as negotiations progressed, ultimately leading to a U.S.-China trade agreement in Geneva. Against this backdrop, the MSCI China Index rose 2.01%, whereas the AMG Veritas China Fund delivered a gain of 0.33%, underperforming the benchmark.

Investment Update

The Fund's performance reflected a mix of strong stock-specific contributions and continued headwinds from select holdings. On the positive side, NetEase (NTES)(OTCPK:NETTF) Games, CSPC Pharmaceutical Group (OTCPK:CSPCY), and Innovent Biologics (OTCPK:IVBIY) were key contributors. NetEase benefited from resilient gaming revenues, supported by strong content pipelines and increased monetisation across core franchises. The company also continued to expand its presence in music and other digital entertainment services, further diversifying its growth profile. CSPC Pharmaceutical delivered steady gains, underpinned by robust performance in its innovative drug portfolio and consistent execution in both domestic and international markets. Innovent Biologics (OTCPK:IVBIY) advanced as new product launches and progress in its biologics pipeline reinforced its position as a leading player in China's biopharma sector. Conversely, BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) and Alibaba (BABA) weighed on returns. BYD came under pressure as investors took profits following strong prior gains, despite its continued leadership in electric vehicles and battery technology. Alibaba declined amid heightened competitive pressures in e-commerce and investor caution around profitability, even as the company continues to focus on restructuring and long-term margin improvement.

Looking Ahead: Innovation and Domestic Resilience

We believe China's long-term growth story continues to shift toward high-value, innovation-led industries. Advances in health care, premium consumer segments, and technology reflect the country's strategic move away from low-cost manufacturing toward self-reliant, IP-driven growth. Meanwhile, policy initiatives are increasingly supportive of strategic sectors, and a softer U.S. Dollar backdrop has helped improve liquidity conditions across the region.

Outlook and Positioning

While short-term volatility is likely to persist given ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties, we remain confident in the structural trends supporting our portfolio. Our focus remains on high-quality businesses with strong innovation capabilities, scalable business models, and robust balance sheets—companies we believe are best positioned to lead China's next phase of growth.

data.title: AMG Veritas Asia Pacific Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG Veritas Asia Pacific Fund (Class N) returned 17.93% for the second quarter of 2025, beating the 12.81% return for its primary benchmark, the MSCI (MSCI) AC Asia Pacific ex Japan Index. For the 12-month period ending June 30, 2025, the Fund returned 14.83%, compared with a 15.84% return for the Index.

Market Overview

Asian markets delivered strong gains in the second quarter of 2025, rebounding from an initially volatile backdrop marked by U.S.-China trade tensions and tariff escalations. As negotiations progressed and the U.S. Dollar weakened, liquidity conditions improved sharply across the region, supporting a broad recovery in equities. The MSCI Asia Pacific ex Japan Index rose 12.81% over the quarter, while the AMG Veritas Asia Pacific ex Japan Fund advanced 17.93%, outperforming by 5.1 percentage points. This strong result reflects our commitment to investing in high-quality businesses with durable structural growth drivers, resilient balance sheets, and clear competitive advantages.

Investment Review

The Fund's outperformance was driven primarily by holdings in Korea such as HD HYUNDAI ELECTRIC, Hanwha Aerospace, and HD Hyundai Heavy Industries. HD HYUNDAI ELECTRIC delivered strong returns, benefiting from robust global demand for power equipment and electrification solutions. As grid modernisation and renewable integration accelerate, the company continues to strengthen its competitive positioning through technological upgrades and global expansion. Hanwha Aerospace performed well, driven by sustained global demand for advanced defence systems and strong export growth in the Middle East. The company's expanding backlog and improving margins underscore its competitive strengths and long-term earnings visibility. HD Hyundai Heavy Industries also contributed positively, supported by healthy demand across shipbuilding, offshore, and energy-related projects. The firm is benefitting from structural tailwinds in energy security and fleet renewal, positioning it as a critical enabler of global trade and energy infrastructure. Eternal Limited advanced on the back of stable earnings growth and strong market share in its niche specialty chemicals and materials businesses. The company's disciplined execution and innovative product development continue to support consistent performance. Pro Medicus added to returns, reflecting solid demand for its advanced imaging software solutions. The business benefits from a growing global health care digitalization trend, with increasing adoption among major hospital networks and radiology groups. The company's focus on scalable, high-margin software underpins its strong earnings trajectory and long-term growth potential.

Conversely, a few holdings detracted from performance. Alibaba (BABA) came under pressure as investors digested mixed signals on China's macro-outlook and ongoing regulatory scrutiny. While near-term sentiment remains cautious, we continue to believe in Alibaba's strong market leadership and long-term growth prospects driven by cloud and artificial intelligence (AI) services. Varun Beverages Limited saw weakness despite solid underlying fundamentals, as investors rotated out of Indian consumer growth stocks. We maintain conviction in its strong franchise, operational excellence, and long-term growth potential supported by rising per capita beverage consumption in India. Telix Pharmaceuticals Ltd (OTCPK:TLPPF) gave back some early gains as the market absorbed conservative forward guidance that excluded potential near-term product approvals. The company remains well positioned for long-term growth with a strong pipeline and continued operational progress. Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY)(OTCPK:TCTZF) Holdings Limited faced headwinds amid concerns around domestic gaming regulation and macro uncertainty in China. Nonetheless, its strong ecosystem, diversified revenue streams, and investments in new growth areas provide a robust foundation for future recovery. BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) also detracted slightly as investors took profits following a period of strong performance. We retain conviction in its leadership in electric vehicles and battery technology, which underpins its long-term global growth opportunity.

Looking Ahead: The Return of Growth and Quality

The quarter highlighted what we believe is an early-stage reversal in market style leadership, with growth and quality regaining favour after a prolonged period dominated by value. The weakening U.S. Dollar has supported liquidity conditions across Asia, and we are seeing clear evidence of renewed appetite for quality growth names. Structural themes such as the global AI capital expenditure cycle, the push towards onshoring and supply chain self-sufficiency, and China's evolving focus on intellectual property-driven growth provide a supportive backdrop for our portfolio. In particular, Korean and Taiwanese technology hardware leaders stand to benefit disproportionately from increasing demand for AI and edge computing infrastructure. China's transition towards higher-value IP exports, particularly in biotech and electric vehicles, continues

to gain traction, as evidenced by landmark licensing deals and growing international consumer brand recognition.

Outlook and Positioning

While geopolitical and macro uncertainties remain, we believe that the longer-term opportunity set in Asia is becoming increasingly attractive. Our focus remains firmly on identifying high-quality, structurally advantaged businesses capable of compounding through economic and policy cycles. As growth and quality styles reassert themselves, we expect our portfolio to continue benefiting from these evolving market dynamics.

data.title: SPYD Passes The 4-Point Check: Dividend, Price Stability, Costs And Scenario
data.description:
Let's be honest … SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SPYD) is not competitive compared to the S&P 500 in terms of total return. At the same time, although it is rich in sectors considered defensive, the drawdowns are still pronounced. At this point, we could say that we do it for the income, but if that were the case, why not fill the portfolio with Treasuries?

They have the same yield. Well, after this list of negative elements, you will be surprised to know that my thesis on SPYD is bullish. Not only do I think that SPYD can have a nice role in an income-oriented portfolio, but also that the economic-financial environment we are entering accentuates this thesis. But let me first say …

What is SPYD

If I tell you that SPYD is simply an income generator and not a vehicle to grow capital, am I being trivial? A bit. But in the end, that is what it tries to be. Through a passive replication technique with sampling, it tracks the S&P 500 High Dividend Index benchmark, with semiannual rebalancing and reconstitution (January and July). A benchmark that, simply put, selects the companies with the highest dividend yield. To complicate it slightly, the methodology is to select the best by (last dividend × annual frequency ÷ price at rebalance) within the S&P 500. It does so as a RIC, and distributes at least 90% of taxable income.

Key features

When it comes to income, there are 4 things I look for in a fund, and honestly SPYD has them all:

  • It has a lean and transparent structure

  • It has both low costs and negligible spread

  • A competitive yield (albeit volatile)

  • An underlying trend in line with the US equity value segment

But let's go in order: transparency simply lies in SPYD's passive replication structure. This element in turn influences a low TER, equal to 0.07%, and keeps the bid/ask spread really tight. Then it must be said that although the dividend tends not to be exactly stable, today it shows 4.7%, while the underlying maintains an upward linear trend.

What's inside?

83 stocks, with a maximum weight of ~1.9% and an annual turnover of 29%.

With an average market cap of $51.3 billion, SPYD naturally has an orientation toward large caps. Compared to the S&P 500, the dividend filter lowers the forward P/E to 14.6 (the S&P 500 is above 20x). Simply put, it's about a value tilt, meaning a strong exposure to value stocks. And this is also evident from the expected EPS growth of only 5.8%, well below growth indexes.

In fact, over 38% of the fund is in Real Estate + Utilities, both very interest-rate sensitive. This explains why SPYD tends to suffer during rate hike phases. This happens because SPYD uses a purely quantitative selection on dividend yield, meaning there are no quality filters (ROE, debt, payout sustainability).

But despite this, it has managed to stand out from its peers.

Peers

There are endless funds of this type, and more will continue to be launched. Here's my selection:

I'll start by saying that SPYD's 3Y performance is disappointing;

however, extending the time horizon to 5Y, there is a clear overperformance.

Simply, it depends on the economic context. SPYD is more value oriented, rich in segments typically underweighted such as real estate and energy. Sectors that suffer from aggressive monetary policies like those post-2022. The other side of the coin? On dividends there is no competition, SPYD offers the highest solution.

And note: if you look at the dividend grade, it would have the lowest rating, and the reason is that SPYD's selection method is not dividend growth oriented.

And this is often a criticism against this fund, but let me say that if the core of SPYD is precisely to select companies with the highest dividend, if there were positive dividend growth in SPYD it would mean that there is a flaw in the benchmark replication methodology. So all regular in my view.

What happens if you apply filters? Theoretically you avoid value traps. A comparison with SCHD, DGRO or NOBL, can SPYD help us find better solutions? Yes and no.

In the sense that in reality, the logic driving returns is the same. Both the aristocrat filter (NOBL) and growth with DGRO lead to a less competitive selection in terms of yields, less exposed to the sectors that characterize SPYD, and therefore less sensitive to changes in the monetary cycle, but also less profitable in terms of income.

Macro switch and risk

It becomes intriguing when you think about the monetary cycle the Fed has entered. If interest rates go down, SPYD can benefit (Utilities + leveraged REITs). It could even give SPYD a boost compared to its peers, given that it had lost traction over the last 3 years.

At the same time, I would say this is a different situation from what might have been the case after the 2020 rate cut, when SPYD started its overperformance. What's different? Simply, here inflation is already high, and market credit conditions come from high rates, not already low rates. This means that purchasing power has eroded a lot and continues to do so, while credit conditions remain more or less tight.

If this is a preemptive cut ahead of an economic contraction, it would not be positive for SPYD's key sectors and, certainly, it would not work as a defensive asset.

Conclusion

To conclude, however, I think that SPYD is a strong instrument for an income-oriented portfolio, because it holds its value well, offering competitive dividends compared to peers at accessible costs and in an economic context that in my opinion remains stimulating today.

data.title: Wall Street Slip: A Closer Look At The Dow, S&P 500, And Nasdaq Decline
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The U.S. stock market slipped on Wednesday, just a day after it had closed higher for three straight sessions. Traders seemed to be weighing what Fed Chair Jerome Powell said, and they also were waiting for more data later in the week.

Investors are moving carefully because the central bank tries to juggle inflation worries while a softening job market looms. Powell noted on Tuesday that asset prices look fairly overvalued. His peers split on the policy path, and the Chair kept stressing the tightrope the Fed must walk.

The S&P 500 energy sub‑index rose about 2%, helped by rising crude oil prices. Consumer discretionary stocks nudged up 0.5%. Heavy‑weight tech, however, slipped around seven‑tenths of a percent, with Apple (AAPL) and Nvidia (NVDA) each losing just over 1%.

Data posted Wednesday showed new‑home sales for single‑family houses jumped 20.5% in August, far above expectations. If the housing market is starting to regain some life, the Fed might read that as a sign there’s less room to lower rates.

US-listed Chinese firms also climbed, led by an almost nine percent jump in Alibaba (BABA) after it announced a tie‑up with Nvidia.

Lithium Americas (LAC) shares nearly doubled after a report said the government might seek a ten percent equity stake. Talks are ongoing for a loan over $2.26 billion to fund the Thacker Pass project with General Motors (GM), which itself rose a little over 1%. UBS even upgraded GM to “buy” from “neutral”.

Micron Technology (MU) fell four percent after its quarterly numbers came out. Oracle (ORCL) slipped three and a half percent after a story that the company plans to raise fifteen billion dollars in bonds.

Freeport‑McMoRan (FCX) tumbled eleven point seven percent, citing lower copper and gold sales for the third quarter.

Overall, the market shows mixed signals - optimism in some sectors, caution in others - and investors seem to be waiting for clearer direction. Time will tell what's next.

A Case for the Rally to Continue

There has been a lot of talk around valuations of late, with Fed Chair Powell having his say as well.

However, looking at research by Goldman Sachs, the S&P 500 is trading near fair value, and earnings growth is set to underpin further upside as the economy gains momentum into next year.

On the flip side, most S&P 500 sectors are valued above their historical averages. This indicates optimism around earnings and macroeconomic stability, though it also heightens the risk of a pullback if growth falters.

Given the concerns around growth in the second half of the year, this may be something that could come into play as we gear up for earnings season once more.

However, if the AI wave continues to drive on, that could offset losses in some sectors, especially when it comes to US indexes.

Looking at the short term, the end of September is usually a bad time for US stocks. This was also the case for September in general, but markets have shrugged that off this year. The question now heading into the last week of the month is whether a pullback will materialize.

Looking Ahead

Market participants will now focus on personal consumption expenditures data, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, due for release later this week.

There is also a host of Fed speakers sharing their thoughts on monetary policy moving forward, as well as University of Michigan sentiment data which will be released on Friday.

Technical Analysis - Nasdaq 100

From a technical perspective, the Nasdaq 100 has had a change of structure on the four-hour chart.

The index has now printed a lower high and lower low but has run into the ascending trendline from the beginning of September.

This does leave the Nasdaq in an interesting position for the rest of the week.

The period-14 RSI has dipped below the 50 level, which also signals a shift to bearish momentum.

Immediate resistance rests at 24667 before the all-time high comes back into focus.

Keep an eye on the RSI. If it moves back above the 50 level, it could be seen as a sign that bulls have returned and fresh highs may be on the horizon.

A trendline break opens up the potential for a deeper retracement toward the 24200 before the confluence level at 24000 comes into focus.

data.title: Invesco Floating Rate ESG Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

ESG integration

The fund actively uses an environmental, social and governance (ESG) overlay in its investment process.

Full cycle view

We position the fund for the long term, a full market cycle. We augment our long-term perspective with an active management approach and the application of several proprietary models that are built to help us exploit shorter term relative value opportunities.

Potential risk mitigation

Due to their senior secured status in the capital structure, loans have the highest priority to be repaid and lenders have first right to collateral in the event of a default. This has historically led to relatively higher recovery levels in the case of defaults.1,2

Manager perspective and outlook

  • The S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index returned 2.33% in the second quarter. 1
  • The start of the second quarter saw heightened market volatility on the heels of President Trump's sweeping tariff policy roll-out, spurring investors to become much more risk averse. Loan prices fell in sympathy, but effects were relatively short-lived. As the market digested the news and loan conditions improved, loan prices and the yield spread between loans and Treasuries stabilized to levels before the tariff announcement. Second quarter issuance of collateralized loan obligations (CLO) was strong, with gross origination of $102.9 billion. 2 Excluding refinancings and resets, CLO issuance was $50.7 billion, about the same as the first quarter. 2 By quarter end, the US leveraged loan market hit a record size of $1.5 trillion, having expanded over 7% in the last year. 3
  • As in prior quarters, credit fundamentals held up; the default rate remained below the historical average and issuer leverage, interest coverage and cash balances remained, in our view, healthy. The difference between the best and worst performing sectors was 2.11%. 1 The par-weighted loan default rate was 1.11% (excluding distressed exchanges) for the quarter, up from 0.82% at the end of last quarter. 1
  • With seemingly favorable consumer sentiment, easing geopolitical risks and continued demand, we believe the environment supports improving loan market conditions.

Portfolio positioning

The fund's core investment process is grounded in a fundamental bottom-up risk assessment of each investment, coupled with top-down risk positioning tied to broader macroeconomic trends.

At quarter end, the fund's largest overweights were in chemicals, telecommunications and transportation, unchanged from last quarter.

Regarding the chemicals sector, the specter of rising tariffs presents, in our view, obvious risks to the global economy, inflation and supply chain integrity. Fortunately, most of the fund's issuers in the chemicals space have local-for-local production, which we believe should insulate them from direct tariff impact. Still, disrupted trade flows could create supply gluts in certain regions and could hamper demand generally.

The fund's chemicals overweight historically tends to be concentrated in well capitalized, highquality businesses with good competitive moats and a clear need to exist.

The fund's largest underweights were in information technology (IT), health care and, to a much lesser extent, food/tobacco. Food/tobacco replaced financials as a top underweight during the quarter.

The IT underweight reflects our concern about excessive valuations that led to elevated leverage levels and constrained free cash flow, which in our view became particularly problematic amid higher interest rates. Even after several rate cuts in 2024, we believe this remains an issue. Many of these businesses have struggled with the transition to cloud-based solutions and subscription-based revenue models, which further constrained cash flows. We are closely managing the fund's IT positions to be sure risk-adjusted return profiles remain, in our view, attractive.

The fund's persistent health care underweight reflects our concerns about reimbursement rates, wage-related cost pressures and a highly politicized nature. Earnings and profit margin profiles have remained mixed and providers have benefited from a better labor mix as labor headwinds have eased. However, increases in reimbursement rates have generally not kept pace with wage inflation, and we expect providers will look for ways to optimize staffing and cost bases. Reimbursement and overall regulatory risk have been an ongoing overhang for health care. If economic conditions deteriorate, we could see further pressure on utilization rates and margins. We maintain a favorable view of health care issuers that have considerable market share positions, solid demand for their services and adequate liquidity to sustain margin pressure and free cash flow deficits in the near to medium term.

ESG Rating Distribution

As senior loans are private instruments, there is only a small pool of the investable universe that is rated by outside ESG rating providers and as such our ESG rating framework is proprietary in nature and no peer comparisons are available. ESG ratings are based on research and due diligence reviews conducted with the management teams of eligible issuers. Each investment opportunity is scored by the portfolio management team on a scale of 1-5 for risks related to multiple ESG factors under each individual pillar of the ESG framework. The environmental pillar ("E") factors include natural resources, pollution and waste, supply chain impact, and environmental opportunities, the social pillar ("S") factors include workforce, community, product responsibility, and human rights, and the governance pillar ("G") factors include management, shareholders, board of directors, auditors, regulatory issuers, corporate social responsibility strategy, anti-corruption, and business ethics. The factors in each ESG pillar may be updated periodically. ESG ratings are updated at least annually for currently invested issuers. Additional information about the Fund's ESG methodology is available in the Fund's prospectus under "Principal Investment Strategies of the Fund".

The S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index represents tradable, senior-secured, U.S.-dollar-denominated non-investment-grade loans. An investment cannot be made directly in an index.

About Risk

Risks of collateralized loan obligations include the possibility that the collateral securities' distributions won't be adequate to make interest or other payments, the collateral quality may decline in value or default, the collateralized loan obligations may be subordinate to other classes, values may be volatile, and issuer disputes may produce unexpected investment results.

An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer's credit rating.

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The Fund uses an Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) scoring methodology to evaluate securities and may forego some market opportunities available to funds that do not use ESG factors. As a result, the Fund may underperform funds that do not screen or score companies based on ESG factors or that use a different methodology. Information used by the Fund to evaluate ESG factors may not be readily available, complete or accurate, which could negatively impact the Fund's ability to apply its methodology, and in turn its performance. Companies eligible for inclusion in the Fund may not reflect the beliefs or values of certain investors or exhibit positive/favorable ESG factors if different metrics were used to evaluate them.

There is a risk that the value of the collateral required on investments in senior secured floating rate loans and debt securities may not be sufficient to cover the amount owed, may be found invalid, may be used to pay other outstanding obligations of the borrower or may be difficult to liquidate.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

Junk bonds have greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer's credit quality. Junk bond values fluctuate more than high quality bonds and can decline significantly over a short time.

Most senior loans are made to corporations with below investment-grade credit ratings and are subject to significant credit, valuation, and liquidity risk. The value of the collateral securing a loan may not be sufficient to cover the amount owed, may be found invalid or may be used to pay other outstanding obligations of the borrower under applicable law. There is also the risk that the collateral may be difficult to liquidate, or that a majority of the collateral may be illiquid.

The Fund is subject to certain other risks. Please see the current prospectus for more information regarding the risks associated with an investment in the Fund.

data.title: TJX Companies: Business Fundamentally Sound, With Good Drivers Backing (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on The TJX Companies (NYSE:TJX), which I upgraded to a hold rating as fundamentals have gotten a lot better (both traffic and same-store-sales growth), this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. The recent developments, showing not only continued momentum in traffic-led growth but also deeper structural improvements in brand relevance and merchandising strategy, led me to believe TJX could potentially sustain its current premium multiple. I upgrade to buy with a 1-year return target of 10%.

Growth outlook

At a high level, the narrative is no longer simply about navigating a challenged consumer backdrop, but one that is about TJX being able to consistently print comparable sales growth [CSG] (the most recent results saw 4% y/y consolidated CSG). This also marked the 9th consecutive quarter of positive CSG. But what matters most are the underlying drivers. The growth was driven entirely by higher customer transactions across every division, not by ticket or pricing. Now, this is a very important dynamic that readers should note because transactions, unlike tickets or pricing, signal genuine demand. Over the recent quarters, with traffic consistently growing, it shows that more shoppers are choosing TJX, and that by that definition, it also means that TJX is addressing a larger pool of consumers (i.e., more volume), which is a major de-risking trend as it validates the point that the TJX brand is still relevant to consumers (i.e., its value proposition is still working very well).

Looking ahead, what further strengthens the bull case is the customers that TJX is capturing—young consumers. Management noted that new customer acquisition continues to skew younger, and beyond all the traffic trends that we have seen, I seriously think this is the most important development for TJX’s long-term investment case. If we look across the retail industry history, I think one of the reasons why so many brands failed to sustain themselves is because they failed to connect with the next generation and gradually became irrelevant as their core customer aged out. As such, it was very encouraging to see TJX gaining good momentum here.

By resonating with younger shoppers today, TJX is building up loyalty and brand relevance—nurturing the idea of the “treasure hunt” proposition with consumers who are entering their prime earning and spending years. This goes beyond just the next CSG performance, but one that would secure decades of relevance and ensure the TJX brand can last for the next 20–30 years. This fundamentally de-risks the business and also provides a long runway of future revenue growth. Unlike temporary promotions or pricing tactics, I believe this is a structural advantage and is one that compounds over time as young shoppers become core customers.

HomeGoods

My initial view on the HomeGoods segment, which I previously noted as a pleasant surprise, has evolved into something far more positive. I believe it is now evident that this banner’s strength is a structural driver that should make TJX’s growth story look even better. The fact that this banner was able to deliver 5% y/y CSG in 2Q26 proves that HomeGoods' value-oriented assortment resonates deeply with consumers even in a discretionary category. Remember that discretionary spending is the category that is facing the most headwinds because of the uncertain US macro landscape.

I’d say this CSG trend should persist as it is driven by a deliberate merchandising strategy that has effectively repositioned stores as year-round destinations. The key distinction here is the fundamental reason why a customer decides to visit. Traditionally, a home retailer’s traffic has been project-based and seasonal. Shoppers come in with a specific mission, like redecorating a room, replacing bedding, or shopping for holiday décor. These are high-intent but infrequent trips. Once the project is completed or the season passes, the customer has little incentive to return for weeks or months, leaving traffic tied to cyclical peaks and troughs. TJX has effectively flipped this model. The addition of categories like gifting and consumables (think gourmet snacks, pantry items, and unique small-ticket goods) fundamentally changes the customer journey. These are not project-specific purchases, as they are tied to recurring needs such as birthdays, thank-yous, or everyday indulgences. This ensures shoppers have a constant reason to stop by.

The result is a new HomeGoods model. Rather than being a store customers engage with occasionally, it becomes part of their regular shopping cadence. This layered on top of the core home offering drives more predictable, recurring traffic while smoothing out the seasonal volatility.

Inventory

The buying environment is also working in favor of TJX, as management described the marketplace as outstanding, and TJX has leaned in aggressively, with per-store inventories up 10% y/y. Previously, I saw rising inventory as a risk, but I think with TJX addressing a larger customer pool and its HomeGoods banner becoming less cyclical, shoring up more inventories should be seen as an offensive strategy, as this strengthens the customer purchasing experience (more goods to choose from) while also simultaneously providing margin protection (inventories bought at an attractive price).

Valuation

I guess the biggest question is whether TJX can continue to trade at a premium multiple—the very reason I did not upgrade to buy previously. As I looked at how TJX has performed (CSG mostly driven by traffic), the progress it has made in de-risking the long-term brand relevance, and also how it has turned the HomeGoods banner around, I see a case for TJX's multiple to continue trending up so long as this momentum continues. The reality is the market is willing to continue attaching a higher multiple (NTM PE has gone up from 24x in June to 28.5x today). If TJX does manage to accelerate earnings growth (Burlington Stores (BURL) EPS is expected to grow at high teens), I think there is a good chance for multiples to continue trending up to 30x (the +2 std dev over the past 5 years) in the near future. 30x NTM PE on FY27 EPS of $5 would equate to $150 (~10% total return if we include ~1% dividend yield). With rates coming down, I think a 1-year upside of 10% is not that bad.

Risk

The risk to my thesis is TJX's valuation. The stock is without a doubt priced for flawless execution, leaving little room for error. Should any execution missteps occur, or consumers' preferences change drastically in the near term, it may cause TJX to miss expectations. There is also a known margin headwind in 2H26, which management’s 3Q guidance reflects. If the actual cost pressure is more than expected, it could lead to TJX disappointing expectations as well. This could lead to a significant multiple contraction.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I believe TJX’s investment case has gotten better. The consistent traffic gains, the success in attracting younger shoppers, and the transformation of HomeGoods into a structural driver are all positive. While valuation risk remains the key overhang, I think the premium multiple is justified so long as execution continues to deliver. With comps improving, inventory positioning favorable, and secular growth levers intact, my rating for TJX is a buy.

data.title: DSL Has Appealing Emerging Market Fixed Income Exposure
data.description:
The DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund (NYSE:DSL) is a closed-end fund [CEF] designed to provide investors with high current income and capital appreciation by investing in high-yield and investment-grade debt issuances. The strategy pays out a distribution of $1.32/share, yielding 10.69% on a forward-looking basis, and has an expense ratio of 2.28%.

About DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund

DSL was launched on April 26, 2013 by DoubleLine Capital. The strategy was designed to provide investors with global fixed income exposure, investing in debt and income-producing investments in order to generate yield. The strategy has approximately $1.4b in net assets, leveraged at 20.96% for gross assets of $1.77b.

The fund pays out a robust monthly distribution rate annualized at $1.32/share, yielding 10.69% on a forward basis. Monthly distributions have been relatively consistent over the years, providing investors with a certain degree of income stability when invested in the strategy. DSL’s most recent distribution was derived mostly from net investment income, accounting for 73% of the distribution, with the remaining 27% deriving from return of capital. DSL can be best utilized by income-oriented investors, or those depending on regular distributions for regular income.

DSL is heavily weighted in emerging markets at 38.40%, bank loans at 20.71%, and high-yield corporates at 20.17%. Roughly 73.52% of the portfolio holdings are below investment-grade issuances, adding a certain degree of income-yielding risk to the portfolio strategy. The majority of issuances held in the portfolio are short-to-intermediate term issuances, making up roughly 82% of the portfolio assets. DSL has relatively low duration risk with an average duration of 5.38 years, making the strategy moderately sensitive to interest rate policy.

DSL is broadly diversified across 483 holdings, with the top 10 accounting for roughly 11.45% of the total portfolio weight, suggesting that no single issuance will significantly impact the performance of the strategy.

DSL may be positioned for strong performance going forward as the US Federal Reserve aims to ease interest rates. On September 17, 2025, the Fed reduced the overnight funds rate to 4.00-4.25%, down from 4.25-4.50%, making this the first rate cut since the end of 2024.

Given the portfolio’s concentration in emerging market issuances, I believe investors can expect a certain degree of positive performance resulting from the US rate cut. Roughly 63% of foreign issuances are denominated in US dollars, also known as Dollar Bonds, meaning that the issuers, not the bonds, may be sensitive to changes in US interest rate policy. At a high level, if the US dollar were to depreciate against the issuer’s native currency, the cost of servicing the debt will decline relative to the foreign exchange rate without impacting the nominal value of the serviceable debt. This means that it may cost the issuer less to service their loans while the cash flows of the dollar bonds remain the same. In aggregate, we can observe the relative value of the US dollar against a basket of foreign currencies in accordance with the US Dollar Index (DXY).

In terms of value to the bondholders, we can potentially expect the foreign issuances to appreciate in value as a result of the recent rate cut. Though the Fed Dot Plot doesn’t necessarily have sway on interest rate policy, it may shed some light into the sentiment towards additional cuts. Reviewing the Dot Plot, we can potentially expect two additional cuts for the remainder of 2025.

The market is certainly expecting further rate cuts with the probability of an October 2025 cut significantly increasing following the September 2025 FOMC meeting, estimating rates to be reduced to 3.75-4.00%. If this were to occur, I suspect the holdings in DSL may benefit, resulting in modest repricing of issuances. Though rate cuts may benefit the strategy, the low duration profile of the fund may limit the upside potential to repricing.

Considering peer strategies, DSL is moderately priced with an expense ratio of 228bps.

In terms of performance, DSL has historically underperformed its peers, particularly when considering the performance of the PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY). PTY provides a substantially lower fee of 119bps and is substantially more diversified across 943 holdings.

Distributions across peer strategies are relatively similar, allowing for potential investors to be selective amongst income strategies. Though PTY pays out a relatively lower distribution in terms of yield, I believe investors should take into consideration the total returns of the fund and not distributions alone.

Risks Related to DSL

DSL invests in global fixed income securities, exposing the strategy to certain risks that investors should be aware of. DSL’s broad regional diversification may expose investors to both domestic and foreign interest rate policy, monetary policy, regional inflation rates and GDP growth, and currency risk, amongst others. Emerging market issuance could potentially be negatively impacted if the US dollar were to rebound and appreciate in value.

High-yield issuances may be sensitive to market dynamics that aren’t exhibited in investment-grade issuances given the higher-risk nature of the securities. In general, high-yield issuances exhibit a certain degree of correlation with broader market indices, potentially making them sensitive to market volatility.

Final Thoughts

DSL may be tactically positioned to benefit from the easing rate policy set by the Federal Reserve. Despite the easing policy environment, DSL may exhibit some market risk given the exposure to high-yield issuances. Given historical performance relative to peers and the higher management fee, I am recommending DSL with a Hold rating.
